title: LiPS: Lightweight Panoptic Segmentation for Resource-Constrained Robotics
abstract: 全景分割是实现机器人感知的关键技术，它将语义理解与物体级推理相统一。然而，当前先进模型日益增加的复杂性使其难以部署在移动机器人等资源受限平台上。我们提出了一种名为LiPS的创新方法，通过轻量化设计解决高效全景分割的挑战，在保留基于查询的解码机制的同时，引入了精简的特征提取与融合路径。该方法旨在提供强大的全景分割性能，同时显著降低计算需求。在标准基准测试上的评估表明，LiPS达到了与复杂模型相当的精度，同时实现了高达4.5倍的每秒帧数处理速度提升，计算量减少近6.8倍。这种高效性使LiPS成为连接现代全景分割模型与现实世界机器人应用的重要桥梁。
html_content:
LiPS: Lightweight Panoptic Segmentation
for Resource-Constrained Robotics
Abstract
Panoptic segmentation is a key enabler for robotic perception, as it unifies semantic understanding with object-level reasoning. However, the increasing complexity of state-of-the-art models makes them unsuitable for deployment on resource-constrained platforms such as mobile robots. We propose a novel approach called LiPS that addresses the challenge of efficient-to-compute panoptic segmentation with a lightweight design that retains query-based decoding while introducing a streamlined feature extraction and fusion pathway. It aims at providing a strong panoptic segmentation performance while substantially lowering the computational demands. Evaluations on standard benchmarks demonstrate that LiPS attains accuracy comparable to much heavier baselines, while providing up to 4.5 higher throughput, measured in frames per second, and requiring nearly 6.8 times fewer computations. This efficiency makes LiPS a highly relevant bridge between modern panoptic models and real-world robotic applications.
Index Terms
—
Panoptic segmentation, Efficient vision transformers, Resource-constrained robotics
1
Introduction
Panoptic segmentation brings together semantic and instance segmentation into a single pixel-wise representation, distinguishing between things (countable objects) and stuff (amorphous regions such as road or sky)
[
8
]
.
This unified view is particularly valuable in robotics, where perception systems must jointly support global scene understanding and object-level reasoning for tasks such as semantic mapping, object-aware planning, and instance-level data association.
Recent progress has been driven by query-based transformers, which cast segmentation as a set prediction problem where learned queries interact with image features through attention to predict masks and categories. Mask2Former
[
2
]
has become a widely adopted representative of this paradigm. However, query-based segmentation models remain computationally and memory intensive due to high-capacity backbones, dense multi-scale features, and costly pixel-level fusion, causing significant latency and energy consumption
[
14
]
. Such computational demands make these models unsuitable for embedded platforms commonly used in robotics, such as Jetson-class devices, which operate under strict power constraints.
To takle this limitations, we propose LiPS (Lightweight Panoptic Segmentation) as an efficient query-based panoptic segmentation method designed for resource-constrained deployment. LiPS preserves the masked transformer decoding paradigm of Mask2Former while streamlining the upstream feature pathway. Concretely, LiPS combines: (i) a compact hierarchical encoder, (ii) a selective feature-routing mechanism that forwards only a subset of pyramid levels, and (iii) a lightweight pixel decoder that fuses the routed features through a shallow, reduced-width deformable-attention pathway. This design directly targets the dominant sources of computation in query-based panoptic pipelines while maintaining strong instance reasoning. Experiments on ADE20K and Cityscapes, conducted on an NVIDIA Jetson AGX Orin with TensorRT FP16 optimization, show that LiPS reduces computation by up to
6.8
×
6.8\times
and improves throughput by up to
4.5
×
4.5\times
compared to Mask2Former, while preserving most of the panoptic quality.
2
Related Work
Fig. 1
:
LiPS architecture. Encoder produces a four-level feature hierarchy. A routing step selects a subset of levels (1-4), which are downsampled with strided convolutions, enriched with sine positional encodings, and fused through a shallow deformable-attention pixel decoder. A lightweight top-down FPN exposes three mask-feature scales for the masked transformer decoder, which predicts class labels and panoptic masks from a fixed set of queries. By default, two routed levels (1 and 2) are used for embedded deployment. Dashed arrows denote skipped levels; solid arrows indicate active information flow.
Query-based transformers have profoundly influenced panoptic segmentation by framing it as a set prediction problem driven by learned queries. Following DETR
[
19
]
, MaskFormer
[
3
]
and, most notably, Mask2Former
[
2
]
established masked transformer decoding over multi-scale feature representations as a prevailing design choice. Building on this paradigm, later works such as kMaX-DeepLab
[
16
]
and OneFormer
[
6
]
, and open-vocabulary panoptic segmentation
[
1
]
introduced improvements in query formulation, decoder design, training strategies, or task unification across segmentation settings.
Across these approaches, query-based decoding assigns masks and semantic labels from backbone features.
Lightweight Vision Transformers such as PVT
[
13
]
, SegFormer
[
15
]
, or AFFormer
[
5
]
demonstrated strong accuracy-efficiency trade-offs in semantic segmentation. In contrast, efficiency in panoptic segmentation received comparatively less attention, with only a few works adapting lightweight backbones to query-based frameworks, e.g., Panoptic SegFormer
[
9
]
or simplifying query-based designs like EoMT
[
7
]
.
3
Our Approach
3.1
Baseline Analysis
We adopt Mask2Former
[
2
]
as our point of departure due to its strong generalization across semantic, instance, and panoptic segmentation. Its architecture can be decomposed into three main components: a hierarchical encoder that extracts multi-scale representations, a pixel decoder that fuses these features, and a masked transformer decoder that performs query-based reasoning. Despite its conceptual elegance, Mask2Former remains computationally demanding even with lightweight backbones such as ResNet-50. Stage-wise profiling of the model reveals a strong imbalance in computational cost, with the encoder accounting for 31.6% of the total GFLOPs, the pixel decoder dominating at 53.7%, and the masked transformer decoder contributing only 5.5%, while the remaining computation is attributed to the final prediction head. This analysis underscores a structural inefficiency where most of the computational budget is allocated to dense multi-scale feature extraction and fusion rather than to query-based reasoning.
3.2
Methodology
Based on this observation, we propose LiPS, a lightweight redesign of Mask2Former that preserves its core architectural principles while directly addressing its relevated stage-wise computational imbalance.
Our method relies on three complementary ideas: (i) selecting only a subset of encoder stages, (ii) compressing spatial representations before multi-scale fusion, and (iii) simplifying the pixel decoder in depth and width. The general architecture of LiPS is illustrated in Figure
1
.
Encoder and Feature Routing.
LiPS employs AFFormer
[
5
]
as a compact hierarchical encoder producing four feature levels with progressively reduced spatial resolution. In contrast to conventional architectures that propagate the full feature pyramid, LiPS employs a routing mechanism to forward only a subset of encoder levels to the pixel decoder. By default, the two highest-resolution stages are selected, as they retain most of the spatial cues required for small-object delineation while remaining computationally inexpensive. To further reduce the token budget, the routed feature maps are spatially compressed prior to fusion using strided
3
×
3
3\times 3
convolutions. Each selected encoder level is uniformly downsampled with progressively smaller stride factors from coarse to fine resolutions (
×
3
\times 3
,
×
2
\times 2
,
×
2
\times 2
,
×
2
\times 2
), reducing both spatial resolution and channel dimensionality. This operation lowers both spatial resolution and channel dimensionality while preserving the hierarchical structure of the features.
Lightweight Pixel Decoder.
The routed features are fused through a top-down deformable-attention pixel decoder
[
19
]
. LiPS introduces two efficiency-oriented design changes. First, the depth of the deformable transformer encoder is reduced from 6 to 3 layers. Second, the channel width of the convolutional adapters is reduced from 256 to 128. Since convolutional cost scales quadratically with channel dimension, this change decreases both parameters and computational complexity by a factor of four. Once fused, the features are passed through a lightweight FPN-style pathway with bilinear upsampling and
1
×
1
1{\times}1
lateral connections, producing compact mask features at
1
/
8
1/8
,
1
/
16
1/16
, and
1
/
32
1/32
input resolutions.
Transformer Decoder.
The masked transformer decoder of Mask2Former is kept unchanged. As shown in the computational analysis, this component is not a bottleneck and has limited impact on latency in embedded settings. Preserving it allows LiPS to maintain the original interaction between queries and masks while isolating the effect of upstream restructuring. Queries interact through self-attention and masked cross-attention over the multi-scale mask features to predict class labels and masks, from which final masks are produced via projection and sigmoid activation.
4
Experiments
4.1
Experimental Setup
We evaluate LiPS on ADE20k
[
18
]
and Cityscapes
[
4
]
to quantify the accuracy-efficiency trade-offs attained by modifying the upstream computational structure while keeping the query-based decoder unchanged. Input image size is fixed to
640
×
640
640{\times}640
for ADE20K and
512
×
1024
512{\times}1024
for Cityscapes.
All experiments are conducted on an NVIDIA Jetson AGX Orin platform (64 GB LPDDR5, Ampere GPU with 2048 CUDA cores and 64 Tensor Cores) configured in MaxN mode (60 W), ensuring realistic embedded deployment conditions.
Panoptic performance is measured using Panoptic Quality (PQ), semantic performance with mean Intersection-over-Union (mIoU), and instance performance with Average Precision (AP). Efficiency is reported in GFLOPs per forward pass (computed with fvcore’s
FlopCountAnalysis
), number of parameters, and throughput in frames per second (FPS) with batch size 1. All evaluations use single-scale inference without test-time augmentation.
To isolate the effect of the restructuring, the masked transformer decoder configuration is kept identical across all variants. Models are implemented in PyTorch, trained following the standard Mask2Former schedule, exported to ONNX, and optimized using TensorRT in FP16 to leverage the Orin’s Tensor Cores.
4.2
Results and Efficiency
4.2.1
Main performance
Tables
1
and
2
evaluate LiPS performance to the Mask2Former baseline. Across both datasets, LiPS (full), which routes all four encoder levels, achieves competitive segmentation quality while requiring only a fraction of the computational cost. On ADE20k, LiPS reaches 36.4
%
\%
PQ and 45.3
%
\%
mIoU at 36.8,GFLOPs, compared to 39.6
%
\%
PQ and 46.0
%
\%
mIoU for Mask2Former-R50 at 147.2 GFLOPs, a reduction of more than 110 GFLOPs for a modest drop of 3.2
%
\%
PQ and 0.7
%
\%
mIoU. A similar trend holds on Cityscapes, where LiPS (full) obtains 55.3
%
\%
PQ at 121.5 GFLOPs, versus 62.1
%
\%
PQ at 527.4 GFLOPs for the baseline, yielding a 6.8
%
\%
PQ gap while cutting computation by over 400 GFLOPs. In terms of throughput, LiPS runs substantially faster, reaching 10.7 FPS on ADE20k and 4.5 FPS on Cityscapes, outpacing Mask2Former despite using less than one quarter of its compute.

4.2.2
Effect of Encoder Routing
We analyze the accuracy-efficiency trade-offs of LiPS by evaluating variants that route different numbers of encoder levels, namely LiPS (1 level), LiPS (2 levels), and LiPS (3 levels). These variants progressively increase the amount of encoder information forwarded to the pixel decoder, thereby controlling both computational complexity and representational capacity. In particular, the one- and two-level configurations achieve similar performance, reaching up to 18.3 FPS on ADE20k while being over six times less complex in terms of GFLOPs. A similar trend is observed on Cityscapes, where LiPS variants reduce computational cost by nearly sevenfold depending on the routing configuration, while running even 4.5 times faster. Hovewer, the one-level variant also exhibits a noticeably larger drop in PQ and AP, making it less suitable when segmentation quality is critical. In contrast, routing two encoder levels recovers most of the accuracy lost by the one-level model while preserving the majority of the computational savings. A closer look at ADE20k (Table
1
), shows that LiPS (2 levels) achieves 35.8
%
\%
PQ at only 26.4 GFLOPs within 0.6
%
\%
PQ of the full LiPS configuration and over 120 GFLOPs lighter than the baseline. A similar pattern appears on Cityscapes (Table
2
), where the LiPS (2 levels) reaches 54.5
%
\%
PQ at 84.2 GFLOPs, remaining close to the full LiPS model while reducing computation by more than 440 GFLOPs. In both datasets, AP is the most sensitive metric to routing depth, whereas PQ and mIoU remain comparatively stable, confirming that most of the representational benefit is already captured with two routed levels. Increasing routing depth to three levels yields a further but modest gain in PQ and mIoU, yet still maintains a significant reduction in GFLOPs compared to Mask2Former-R50.
These results show that most of the panoptic quality is preserved when forwarding only a small subset of encoder stages, and that deeper routing yields diminishing returns relative to its computational cost.
Routing two encoder levels provides the best accuracy-efficiency compromise. On ADE20K, this configuration achieves 35.8
%
\%
PQ and 44.0
%
\%
mIoU at 26.4 GFLOPs, remaining within 0.6
%
\%
PQ of the full LiPS model while reducing the computational cost by a factor of 5.5 compared to Mask2Former. On Cityscapes, it reaches 54.5
%
\%
PQ at 84.2 GFLOPs, reducing computation by more than 440 GFLOPs compared to the baseline. Across both datasets, AP is the most sensitive metric to routing depth, while PQ and mIoU remain comparatively stable, indicating that most semantic and panoptic reasoning is preserved with two routed levels. Qualitative results (Figure
3
) confirm that boundary quality and thing/stuff separation remain visually close to Mask2Former.
4.2.3
Scaling with Image Resolution
Figure
2
studies scaling properties of LiPS with respect to input image size. While Mask2Former exhibits a steep growth in computation, LiPS shows a much flatter scaling behavior. The reduction factor increases with resolution: approximately

. Notably, the two-level configuration requires only 11.6 GFLOPs at

, compared to 52.2 and 79.7 GFLOPs for Mask2Former, respectively. This confirms that suppressing upstream multi-scale feature growth becomes increasingly beneficial at high resolutions, which is particularly relevant for embedded perception with high-resolution sensors.

4.2.4
Ablation studies
Before integrating the simplified pixel decoder into LiPS, we validate its design changes directly on Mask2Former-R50. Table
3
shows that reducing the number of deformable-attention layers from 6 to 3 decreases computation from 147 to 125 GFLOPs and parameters from 44.0M to 41.8M, while PQ drops only from 39.6
%
\%
to 38.7
%
\%
. Further reduction to a single layer significantly impacts PQ (36.9
%
\%
), indicating that a small number of fusion layers is sufficient but cannot be removed entirely.

evaluates channel width reduction. Shrinking the pixel decoder and mask branch from 256 to 128 channels reduces computation from 147 to 97.7 GFLOPs and parameters from 44.0M to 40.7M, with a limited PQ decrease (39.6

5
Discussion
Fig. 3
:
Qualitative comparison on Cityscapes
Columns (left
→
\to
right): (a) input, (b) ground truth,
(c) LiPS (2 levels), (d) LiPS (full),
(e) Mask2Former-R50.
A fixed ROI is cropped and magnified identically across methods to enable like-for-like
inspection of boundary adherence (e.g., curb/sidewalk), thin-structure fidelity (poles, traffic signs),
and stuff/thing separation.
LiPS attains most of its efficiency by compressing feature maps before attention. Strided downsampling of all routed levels reduces the token budget that drives the cost of multi-scale deformable attention. Combined with a shallow fusion stack and a lightweight top-down path, this design concentrates savings where they matter most while leaving the decoder unchanged. Routing fewer encoder levels further removes redundant scales at the source and exposes a direct accuracy-compute trade-off: with only two routed levels, LiPS preserves the bulk of panoptic quality while cutting FLOPs and activation traffic by more than a factor of four.
The main limitation of LiPS lies in instance AP, which decreases when the pyramid is aggressively thinned. On ADE20K and Cityscapes, AP drops by several points (up to about ten depending on the routing configuration), reflecting reduced high-frequency cues needed for small and thin objects. Consequently, the loss in PQ is largely driven by small instances such as poles, distant traffic signs, or other fine structures that are more difficult to recover under heavy compression. Figure
3
illustrates this behavior: while two routed levels remain visually close to deeper settings, occasional misses appear on thin structures and sharp boundaries.
By contrast, mIoU remains comparatively stable, within less than one point of Mask2Former-R50 on ADE20K and within five points on Cityscapes, indicating that semantic segmentation quality is largely unaffected. This resilience makes LiPS also attractive as a semantic segmentation architecture. Compared to transformer-based designs such as Segmenter
[
12
]
or SegViT
[
17
]
, the cost is substantially lower. At
640
×
640
640\times 640
image sizes on ADE20k, Segmenter requires 672 GFLOPs
[
10
]
, and SegViT 624 GFLOPs
[
11
]
, both with ViT-L as backbone. In contrast, LiPS reaches competitive mIoU with far less complexity. It requires up to 25.5 times less compute.
Finally, as LiPS focuses on suppressing dense upstream activations, it remains compatible with other compression techniques including quantization, token pruning, or distillation.
6
Conclusion
We introduced LiPS, a lightweight panoptic segmentation framework that preserves the query-driven decoding paradigm of Mask2Former while streamlining the upstream feature path. LiPS gains efficiency by routing only a subset of encoder levels. These are compressed with strided downsampling and fused through a shallow deformable path with a minimal top-down stage. The decoder for instance-level reasoning remains unchanged. On the evaluated datasets, LiPS reduces computational complexity by a factor of 6.8 in terms of GFLOPs, depending on the input resolution and the number of routed encoder levels selected, and achieves an inference speedup of up to 4.5
×
\times
compared to the baseline. However, two routed levels emerge as a practical default, retaining most of the panoptic quality while offering substantial efficiency gains. These results demonstrate that careful upstream simplification, rather than redesigning the decoder, is sufficient to make panoptic segmentation more tractable under resource constraints. Beyond panoptic segmentation, the stability of semantic mIoU under compression suggests that LiPS can also serve as an efficient semantic segmentation framework, offering a competitive alternative to recent transformer-based designs. Overall, efficiency-oriented architectures such as LiPS make real-time, deployable panoptic segmentation feasible for the first time on resource-constrained platforms, enabling scene understanding in practical robotic and embedded settings.

preview_text:
Panoptic segmentation is a key enabler for robotic perception, as it unifies semantic understanding with object-level reasoning. However, the increasing complexity of state-of-the-art models makes them unsuitable for deployment on resource-constrained platforms such as mobile robots. We propose a novel approach called LiPS that addresses the challenge of efficient-to-compute panoptic segmentation with a lightweight design that retains query-based decoding while introducing a streamlined feature extraction and fusion pathway. It aims at providing a strong panoptic segmentation performance while substantially lowering the computational demands. Evaluations on standard benchmarks demonstrate that LiPS attains accuracy comparable to much heavier baselines, while providing up to 4.5 higher throughput, measured in frames per second, and requiring nearly 6.8 times fewer computations. This efficiency makes LiPS a highly relevant bridge between modern panoptic models and real-world robotic applications.

LiPS: Lightweight Panoptic Segmentation
for Resource-Constrained Robotics
Abstract
Panoptic segmentation is a key enabler for robotic perception, as it unifies semantic understanding with object-level reasoning. However, the increasing complexity of state-of-the-art models makes them unsuitable for deployment on resource-constrained platforms such as mobile robots. We propose a novel approach called LiPS that addresses the challenge of efficient-to-compute panoptic segmentation with a lightweight design that retains query-based decoding while introducing a streamlined feature extraction and fusion pathway. It aims at providing a strong panoptic segmentation performance while substantially lowering the computational demands. Evaluations on standard benchmarks demonstrate that LiPS attains accuracy comparable to much heavier baselines, while providing up to 4.5 higher throughput, measured in frames per second, and requiring nearly 6.8 times fewer computations. This efficiency
one_line_summary: LiPS是一种轻量化的全景分割方法，旨在降低计算需求，适用于资源受限的机器人平台。
